messages.content: I need JWT auth middleware for Express. It should read the token from the Authorization header, verify it, and attach the user to req.user. Return 401 if invalid.

I'll create a middleware that extracts the Bearer token, verifies it with jsonwebtoken, and attaches the decoded payload. I'll also handle token expiry separately from invalid signatures so you can show the right error message.
Good, but also make sure it handles missing headers gracefully — don't crash, just return 401.

Added null checks for missing or malformed Authorization headers. The middleware now returns 401 with a descriptive message for: missing header, wrong format, expired token, and invalid signature — each as a distinct error code.

keyPoints: Bearer token extraction from Authorization header
Separate error codes for expiry vs invalid signature
decisions: Used jsonwebtoken library (RS256)
Error codes: TOKEN_MISSING, TOKEN_EXPIRED, TOKEN_INVALID

messages.content: Write a job queue parser in Python that reads tasks from Redis and dispatches them to worker threads. I need retry logic too.

I'll build a parser that uses BLPOP for blocking reads from Redis, deserializes JSON job payloads, and dispatches to a thread pool. For retries I'll use exponential backoff with a max of 3 attempts, then move failed jobs to a dead-letter queue.
Can the retry count be configurable per job type?
Yes — I'll add a job_type registry where each type specifies max_retries and backoff_factor. The registry is loaded from a YAML config file at startup.

constraints: Must not lose jobs on worker crash
decisions: Used structlog for JSON-structured logs
Exponential backoff: 2^attempt seconds

messages.content: Build a schema validation utility using Zod. It should validate API request bodies and return structured error messages, not throw.
I'll create a validateSchema wrapper around Zod's safeParse that normalises errors into a { field, message, code } array, never throws, and returns a typed Result<T, ValidationError[]>.
The error messages need to be human-readable, not Zod's internal codes.
Added a message map that converts Zod error codes to plain English. Custom messages per field are supported via the schema definition.

constraints: Must not throw — always return Result
Error format must match API spec exactly
decisions: Result type over exception handling
Flat error array for API consumers

messages.content: Implement a secure token generator for API keys. Needs to be cryptographically random, URL-safe, and have a prefix to identify the token type.
I'll use crypto.randomBytes(32) for 256 bits of entropy, encode as base64url for URL safety, and prepend a type prefix like 'pk_live_'.
Can we add a checksum so we can detect accidentally corrupted tokens?
Added a 4-byte CRC32 checksum appended to the token before encoding. validateToken() decodes, strips the checksum, recomputes it, and returns false if it doesn't match.

decisions: CRC32 over full HMAC for lightweight validation
Prefix is part of the token, not stripped on validation

messages.content: Build a webhook handler that validates HMAC signatures and routes events to the right processor.
I'll use crypto.timingSafeEqual to compare the HMAC-SHA256 signature. After validation, events are dispatched to a processor registry keyed by event type.
We need replay attack protection too.
Added a timestamp check: webhooks older than 5 minutes are rejected. Redis stores signatures with a 10-minute TTL to catch duplicates. Falls back to timestamp-only if Redis is unavailable.

constraints: Unknown event types must not error
decisions: Redis replay protection is optional/degrades gracefully
Acknowledge unknown events — never reject them

messages.content: Design the JSON schema for our feature flag config. Flags need to support boolean toggles, percentage rollouts, and per-user overrides.

Here's a schema with three flag types: simple boolean, percentage (0-100 with a seed for consistent hashing), and user list. Each flag has an 'enabled' master switch so you can kill it instantly regardless of type.
Add an 'environments' field so flags can differ between staging and prod.
Added an environments map. Each environment inherits the top-level defaults and can override any field. If an environment key is missing, it falls back to the top-level value.

keyPoints: Three flag types: boolean, percentage, user-list
Master 'enabled' kill switch on every flag
constraints: Schema must be human-editable without tooling
decisions: JSON over YAML for strict typing
Percentage uses consistent hashing on user ID — no DB needed

messages.content: Write a SQL migration to add an audit_log table. It needs to record who changed what, when, and from which IP. Must work on Postgres.

I'll create an audit_log table with: id (uuid), table_name, record_id, action (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE), old_data (jsonb), new_data (jsonb), user_id, ip_address (inet), and created_at. I'll also add a trigger that auto-populates it on any change to watched tables.
The migration needs to be safe to run on a live table with 50M rows.
Using CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS and CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY so it won't lock. The trigger is added last after indexes are built. Added a rollback section that drops the table and trigger cleanly.

keyPoints: audit_log table with jsonb old/new data snapshots
CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY — no table lock
constraints: Must be safe on 50M-row live table
decisions: jsonb over separate columns for flexibility
CONCURRENTLY index to avoid downtime
Trigger approach over application-layer logging

commit.message: feat: audit log migration and trigger
commit.author: Rahul Sharma

requirement.title: Stateless auth migration from sessions to JWT

messages.content: We need to migrate our entire auth system from server-side sessions to stateless JWT. This touches the middleware, token util, session model, and auth config.

I'll plan this as four coordinated changes: 1) Update auth middleware to verify JWTs instead of looking up sessions. 2) Rewrite token util to sign JWTs. 3) Deprecate the session model with a migration flag. 4) Update auth.json with JWT config (secret, expiry, algorithm).
How do we handle users who are currently logged in with sessions during the rollout?

Add a dual-mode period: the middleware checks for a JWT first, falls back to session lookup if not found. The auth.json 'dual_mode' flag controls this. After 30 days, flip the flag and the session fallback path is dead code, ready to remove.

constraints: Zero logout of existing users during rollout
Must be reversible within the 30-day window
decisions: Dual-mode over big-bang cutover
Config flag over code flag for ops control
Session model deprecated-not-deleted until window closes

messages.content: Add rate limiting to our API. Different endpoints need different limits — webhooks should be stricter than user endpoints.

I'll use a sliding window algorithm stored in Redis. The config file defines limits per route pattern. The middleware reads the config, applies the matching rule, and returns 429 with Retry-After on breach.
What if Redis is down?
Added a fail-open mode: if Redis is unreachable, rate limiting is skipped and a warning is logged. A config flag 'fail_closed' can flip this to fail-safe (reject all) for sensitive endpoints.

constraints: Must not block requests if Redis is down (default)
Retry-After must be accurate to the second
decisions: Sliding window over token bucket for burst accuracy
Fail-open default — availability over strict limiting
